perturbation.original_text: Reseller must ensure that Sub-Reseller complies with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller as if Sub- Reseller were an original party to the Agreement and any breach by Sub-Reseller of the Agreement will be deemed a breach by Reseller.
perturbation.changed_text: Reseller is encouraged to ensure that Sub-Reseller complies with the terms of the Agreement applicable to Reseller, but any action or inaction by Sub-Reseller will not be deemed a breach by Reseller.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the direct responsibility of the Reseller for the Sub-Reseller's compliance, weakening enforcement and potentially violating consumer protection laws if the Sub-Reseller fails to uphold required standards. It shifts from 'must ensure' to 'encouraged,' and eliminates the liability for the Sub-Reseller's breaches.
perturbation.law_explanation: By weakening the Reseller's responsibility for Sub-Reseller compliance, this modification could allow Sub-Resellers to engage in deceptive practices without recourse against the Reseller, potentially violating the California Consumer Legal Remedies Act, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices in transactions for goods or services.

CONSUMERS LEGAL REMEDIES ACT [1750 - 1784] ( Title 1.5 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) CHAPTER 1. General Provisions [1750 - 1756] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550. ) 1750. This title may be cited as the Consumers Legal Remedies Act. (Added by Stats. 1970, Ch. 1550.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes 'Reseller must ensure' to 'Reseller is encouraged to ensure' and removes the liability of the Reseller for the Sub-Reseller's breaches. This directly contradicts the original text, weakening the Reseller's responsibility for the Sub-Reseller's compliance. The Law Explanation suggests this could violate the California Consumer Legal Remedies Act, which prohibits unfair or deceptive acts or practices. Scraped Law Snippet 1 indicates that the Consumers Legal Remedies Act exists in California Civil Code Section 1750. The change effectively removes a layer of consumer protection by absolving the Reseller of responsibility for the Sub-Reseller's actions, creating a legal loophole. This aligns with the definition of Omission Legal Contradiction, where a legally mandated consumer protection is omitted, making it non-compliant.

perturbation.original_text: The terms and conditions herein contained constitute the entire agreement between the Parties with respect to the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement and supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: The terms and conditions herein contained constitute the entire understanding between the Parties with respect to the subject matter of this Sub-Reseller Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing 'agreement' to 'understanding' and removing 'supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings, whether oral or written, between the Parties hereto with respect to the subject matter hereof', this clause implies that the Sub-Reseller agreement may not fully protect the consumer due to potential pre-existing agreements between Salesforce and the Reseller.
perturbation.law_explanation: By not superseding previous agreements, the customer warranty protections may not be fully enforceable, violating the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act which aims to improve the adequacy of information available to consumers and prevent deception.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 15 / CHAPTER 50 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 15 USC Ch. 50 : CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES From Title 15—COMMERCE AND TRADE CHAPTER 50 —CONSUMER PRODUCT WARRANTIES Sec. 2301. Definitions. 2302. Rules governing contents of warranties. 2303. Designation of written warranties. 2304. Federal minimum standards for warranties. 2305. Full and limited warranting of a consumer product. 2306. Service contracts; rules for full, clear and conspicuous disclosure of terms and conditions; addition to or in lieu of written warranty. 2307. Designation of representatives by warrantor to perform duties under written or implied warranty. 2308. Implied warranties. 2309. Procedures applicable to promulgation of rules by Commission. 2310. Remedies in consumer disputes. 2311. Applicability to other laws. 2312. Effective dates. §2301. Definitions For the purposes of this chapter: (1) The term "consumer product" means any tangible personal property which is distributed in commerce and which is normally used for personal, family, or household purposes (including any such property intended to be attached to or installed in any real property without regard to whether it is so attached or installed). (2) The term "Commission" means the Federal Trade Commission. (3) The term "consumer" means a buyer (other than for purposes of resale) of any consumer product, any person to whom such product is transferred during the duration of an implied or written warranty (or service contract) applicable to the product, and any other person who is entitled by the terms of such warranty (or service contract) or under applicable State law to enforce against the warrantor (or service contractor) the obligations of the warranty (or service contract). (4) The term "supplier" means any person engaged in the business of making a consumer product directly or indirectly available to consumers. (5) The term "warrantor" means any supplier or other person who gives or offers to give a written warranty or who is or may be obligated under an implied warranty. (6) The term "written warranty" means— (A) any written affirmation of fact or written promise made in connection with the sale of a consumer product by a s
perturbation.justification: The omission of the clause 'supersede any previous and contemporaneous agreements and understandings' weakens consumer warranty protections. The Law Explanation states that this violates the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC Ch. 50, concerns consumer product warranties, which the modified text potentially undermines by not explicitly superseding previous agreements. The original text ensured this agreement was the entire agreement, while the changed text opens the door for previous agreements to take precedence, potentially reducing warranty enforceability, creating a legal contradiction by omission.

perturbation.original_text: This Sub-Reseller Agreement may be executed in one or more counterparts, including facsimiles or scanned copies sent via email or otherwise, each of which will be deemed to be a duplicate original, but all of which, taken together, will be deemed to constitute a single instrument.
perturbation.changed_text: This Sub-Reseller Agreement requires original, physical signatures from both parties to be considered valid.
perturbation.explanation: By requiring original, physical signatures, the clause restricts electronic signatures, potentially creating issues with the validity and enforceability of the agreement, especially considering the prevalence of electronic transactions, which may violate the Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act).
perturbation.contradicted_law: Electronic Signatures in Global and National Commerce Act (E-Sign Act)
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification, requiring physical signatures, contradicts the E-Sign Act which establishes that electronic signatures have the same legal validity as handwritten signatures, potentially hindering the agreement's validity and enforceability in violation of the Act.
perturbation.justification: The changed text mandates physical signatures, which directly opposes the principle of the E-Sign Act that gives electronic signatures the same legal weight as handwritten ones. This requirement introduces a contradiction, as it could invalidate agreements solely based on the absence of physical signatures, undermining the legal standing of electronic signatures. While a law snippet is missing, the perturbation and law explanations clearly outline the conflict with the E-Sign Act, a legally mandated consumer protection that the original text did not violate, but the changed text does, making it non-compliant.
